text: The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, aiming to bring the tissue back to the midline while exposing the ureter. The proximal margin is marked on the soft, pliable bowel without diverticula, and evidence of previous inflammatory disease is visible in the pelvis.
The surgeon continues the dissection, moving up and over the left kidney towards the splenic flexure, which is visible just beyond the ridge.

The surgeon creates a mesenteric window by carefully dissecting through the thickened woody mesentery, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery. This technique involves taking multiple bites to ensure a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon works down to the upper rectum, cleaning off the planned margin for transection. The blood supply is confirmed before using scissors to transect the margin, preparing for transrectal extraction of the specimen.

The surgeon divides the rectosigmoid colon and morselates the specimen into smaller pieces, using a vessel sealer to prevent blood oozing from the vessels. Each smaller piece is extracted sequentially, with the specimen being divided into three pieces.

The surgeon cleans the surgical field and begins sewing an end-to-end anastomosis starting at the posterior midline, taking full thickness bites of the posterior bowel wall. The uterus is resuspended for better visualization, and the surgeon continues around the left lateral aspect of the anastomosis, ensuring healthy bowel edges. Interrupted trio silk sutures are used in areas that require reinforcement.

The surgeon prepares to suture the anterior row of the anastomosis, starting in the posterior midline adjacent to the first suture. The mucosa is aligned precisely, and the surgeon checks perfusion by inspecting the open lumens of the bowel.
The surgeon begins the anterior canal suture, taking it across to the original stitch on the left side. The suture is then brought back towards the midline, avoiding knot tying in the corners.
The surgeon cuts the original suture and ties the two ends together, then oversows the knot area with a 3-0 silk suture to reinforce the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test by driving the scope through the anastomosis, observing no bubbles, indicating a successful seal. The omentum is placed down in the pelvis to cover the anastomosis.
